title: VAT Registration & Return Filing Bangladesh – Business Guide to NBR Compliance
metaTitle: VAT Registration & Return Filing Bangladesh – NBR Guide
metaDescription: Complete guide to VAT registration and return filing Bangladesh : NBR portal, Mushak forms, turnover thresholds, input tax credit, penalties & appeals.
heroIntro: Value Added Tax (VAT) is the backbone of Bangladesh's indirect tax system, contributing over 35% of total national revenue. For businesses operating in Bangladesh, understanding and complying with the VAT and Supplementary Duty Act 2012 is not optional — non-compliance can result in heavy penalties, forced closure, and criminal prosecution. This comprehensive 2026 guide covers everything a business owner needs to know about VAT registration, monthly return filing, input tax credits, and how to handle VAT audits and demand notices from the National Board of Revenue (NBR).

How to Respond to a VAT Demand Notice
VAT Appellate Tribunal: Challenging NBR Decisions

sections.h2: Overview: The VAT and Supplementary Duty Act 2012
sections.content:
Bangladesh's VAT system is governed by the Value Added Tax and Supplementary Duty Act 2012 (VAT Act 2012), which came into full effect on 1 July 2019 after several years of delayed implementation. The Act replaced the outdated VAT Act 1991 and introduced a modern, invoice-based VAT system aligned with international best practices.

Key Concepts Under the VAT Act 2012:

  • Standard VAT Rate: 15% on most goods and services (Section 15)
  • Supplementary Duty (SD): An additional consumption tax on luxury goods, tobacco, alcohol, and petroleum products — rates range from 1% to 500%
  • Truncated Rate: Certain service sectors pay a fixed percentage of supply value as VAT (not the full 15%) — e.g., retail traders may pay at a truncated rate
  • Zero Rate (Export): Exported goods and services are zero-rated (0% VAT) under Section 25, allowing exporters to claim refunds of input VAT paid
  • VAT Exemptions: Certain goods and services are exempt from VAT (listed in the First Schedule of the Act) — primarily agricultural produce, essential medicines, and educational materials

Administering Authority: VAT is administered by the National Board of Revenue (NBR) through its VAT department — specifically the Large Taxpayer Unit (LTU) for large businesses and VAT Commissionerates for others. The NBR's online portal for VAT is vat.gov.bd (also accessible through the integrated government portal).

As of 2026, the NBR has significantly strengthened VAT enforcement through digital means — Electronic Fiscal Devices (EFDs) are now mandatory for most retail and service businesses, automatically recording each transaction and transmitting data to the NBR.

sections.h2: Who Must Register for VAT in Bangladesh?
sections.content:
Whether and how you register for VAT in Bangladesh depends on your annual turnover:

  • Above BDT 3 Crore (30 million) annual turnover: Mandatory full VAT registration. You must collect 15% VAT from customers, file monthly returns (Mushak-9.1), and claim input tax credits.
  • BDT 50 Lakh to BDT 3 Crore annual turnover: Eligible for the Turnover Tax (Package VAT) scheme under Section 64. You pay a simplified flat tax of 4% on turnover (not on VAT base) and file a simplified annual return. This was previously the Mushak-6.3 threshold at BDT 30 lakh, revised upward.
  • Below BDT 50 Lakh annual turnover: Exempt from VAT registration (micro-business exemption). However, if you are in the supply chain of a VAT-registered business, you may still need to register at the buyer's request for input tax credit purposes.

Mandatory Registration Regardless of Turnover: Certain businesses must register for VAT regardless of turnover, including:

  • Importers and exporters
  • Manufacturers in specified sectors (garments, pharmaceuticals, food processing)
  • Businesses supplying to VAT-registered large buyers who need input tax credit
  • Service providers in specified sectors (consulting, legal, accounting, advertising, telecom)
  • Businesses operating in export processing zones (EPZs)

Voluntary Registration: A business below the threshold can voluntarily register for VAT if it benefits from input tax credit claims — for example, a supplier to export-oriented factories.

sections.h2: How to Register for VAT Online at the NBR Portal
sections.content:
VAT registration in Bangladesh is done online through the NBR VAT portal. Here is the step-by-step process:

  1. Access the NBR VAT Portal: Go to vat.gov.bd and click on New Registration. You can also access this through the integrated government services portal.
  2. Business Information Entry: Enter your business details: business name, address, trade license number, TIN (Tax Identification Number), nature of business activities, and HS code (Harmonized System code) for goods or service category code.
  3. Owner/Director Information: Enter the NID and personal details of all proprietors, partners, or directors.
  4. Bank Account Details: Provide the business bank account number and branch for VAT payment verification.
  5. Document Upload: Upload scanned copies of: (a) Trade License, (b) TIN Certificate, (c) NID of proprietor/directors, (d) Bank account details/cancelled cheque, (e) Tenancy agreement or ownership document for business premises, (f) Company incorporation documents (for companies).
  6. Application Submission: Submit the completed application. You will receive an acknowledgment with a reference number.
  7. Field Verification: A VAT Inspector may visit your business premises to verify the information. Currently this process takes approximately 7-15 working days for straightforward applications.
  8. BIN Issuance: Upon approval, you receive a Business Identification Number (BIN) — a unique 9-digit VAT registration number. The BIN Certificate is downloadable from the portal.

Important: Your BIN must be displayed at your business premises and printed on all VAT invoices (Mushak-6.3 challan or Mushak-6.2 tax invoice).

sections.h2: Mushak Forms: The Complete Filing System
sections.content:
The VAT filing system in Bangladesh uses a series of standardized forms called Mushak forms. Understanding which form to use and when is essential for compliance. The key forms currently in use under the VAT Act 2012 are:

FormPurposeWhen Used
Mushak-6.1Tax Invoice for full VAT registrantsEvery sale of goods or services by a full VAT registrant
Mushak-6.2Simplified Tax InvoiceRetail sales where buyer does not need input credit
Mushak-6.3Turnover Tax ChallanBusinesses under the turnover tax scheme
Mushak-6.6Credit Note (debit/credit adjustment)When goods are returned or price adjustments made
Mushak-6.7Debit NoteWhen additional charges are applied after original invoice
Mushak-9.1Monthly VAT ReturnFiled by all full VAT registrants by the 15th of each month
Mushak-4.3VAT Payment ChallanWhen paying VAT to government account at Sonali Bank
Mushak-19Refund ApplicationWhen claiming a VAT refund (e.g., exporters)

Full VAT registrants (BIN holders with turnover above BDT 3 crore) must file a monthly VAT return using Form Mushak-9.1. The return is due by the 15th day of the following month (i.e., the return for June is due by 15 July). This is a strict deadline — filing even one day late attracts penalties.

What the Mushak-9.1 Return Contains:

  • Total output VAT collected from customers during the month
  • Total input VAT paid on purchases (purchases and import duty)
  • Net VAT payable (output VAT minus input VAT credit)
  • Details of all purchase and sale transactions
  • Adjustments for credit notes and debit notes
  • Details of any exempt or zero-rated supplies

How to File: As of 2026, the Mushak-9.1 return must be filed online through the NBR VAT portal (vat.gov.bd). Paper filing is no longer accepted by most commissionerates. After filing, VAT payable must be deposited to the government account through a Treasury Challan (Mushak-4.3) at Sonali Bank or via online banking (IBAS++ system).

Nil Return: If there were no sales during a month, a nil return must still be filed. Failure to file even a nil return is a compliance violation.

Record Keeping: Under Section 66 of the VAT Act 2012, every registered person must maintain VAT account books (purchase register, sales register, input VAT credit register) for a minimum of 5 years from the date of the relevant transaction. These records must be produced on demand during a VAT audit.

sections.h2: Input Tax Credit: How to Recover VAT You Pay on Purchases
sections.content:
The input tax credit (ITC) system is one of the most valuable features of the VAT Act 2012. It allows VAT-registered businesses to recover the VAT they paid on their business purchases, reducing their net VAT liability to the government.

How ITC Works (Example):

  • You purchase raw materials worth BDT 1,00,000 and pay 15% VAT = BDT 15,000 input VAT
  • You manufacture a product and sell it for BDT 2,00,000 and collect 15% VAT = BDT 30,000 output VAT
  • Net VAT payable = BDT 30,000 (output) - BDT 15,000 (input) = BDT 15,000
  • You only pay BDT 15,000 to the government, not the full BDT 30,000

Conditions for Claiming ITC:

  • The purchase must be for business purposes (not for personal use)
  • You must hold a valid tax invoice (Mushak-6.1) from the supplier showing their BIN
  • The supplier must also be VAT-registered and must have reported the sale in their return
  • The goods or services must not be used for an exempt supply
  • ITC must be claimed in the same month or within 4 months of the purchase

ITC on Capital Machinery: VAT paid on capital machinery and equipment used in production is creditable, subject to conditions. For machinery worth over BDT 10 lakh, the ITC is typically taken over 12 months in equal installments.

ITC for Exporters: Exporters make zero-rated supplies — they charge no VAT to foreign buyers. But they pay input VAT on their local purchases. They are entitled to a VAT refund for this input VAT. Refund claims are filed using Form Mushak-19 and are subject to verification by the VAT Commissionerate. Refund processing currently takes 3-12 months.

sections.h2: VAT Rates by Sector in Bangladesh
sections.content:
Bangladesh applies different VAT rates depending on the sector. Here is a summary of key sectors and their applicable VAT rates as of 2026:

Sector / ActivityVAT RateNotes
Manufacturing (most goods)15%Standard rate
Export of goods and services0% (zero-rated)Full input credit refundable
Restaurant and food service5% (truncated)Applied on supply value; no input credit
Construction (contractors)7.5% (truncated)Applied on contract value
IT services (software, IT enabled)0% (exempt for IT-ITES)Incentive for digital economy
Telecommunication services15% + supplementary dutyMobile talk time: 15% VAT + SD
Real estate (developer sales)2% on flat/apartment saleOn deed value above threshold
Legal and accounting services15%Professional services — standard rate
Essential medicines (listed)0% (exempt)First Schedule — healthcare policy
Tobacco products15% VAT + high supplementary dutySD ranges from 45% to 65%

Note: VAT rates are subject to change in each year's Finance Act/Budget. Always verify current rates with an accountant or tax lawyer before filing.

The VAT and Supplementary Duty Act 2012 imposes significant penalties for non-compliance. NBR VAT enforcement has become increasingly aggressive as of 2026, with digital EFD data allowing the department to identify discrepancies more easily. Key penalties include:

  • Failure to Register (Section 85): Operating without VAT registration when required — penalty of BDT 10,000-50,000 and collection of all VAT due from the date business commenced.
  • Late Filing of Return (Section 86): Penalty of BDT 10,000 for the first month's late filing, plus BDT 1,000 per day for each subsequent day of delay. Interest at 2% per month is also charged on the outstanding VAT amount.
  • False Return or Under-Declaration (Section 90): Penalty of 2-3 times the amount of tax evaded. This is in addition to the principal tax plus interest.
  • Tax Evasion (Section 91): Criminal penalty of 3-5 years imprisonment plus fine equal to the evaded amount. Criminal cases are filed by the VAT Commissionerate in Magistrate Courts.
  • Failure to Issue Tax Invoice (Section 87): Penalty of BDT 5,000-20,000 per violation plus potential business licence suspension.
  • Failure to Maintain Records (Section 88): Penalty of BDT 10,000 for failure to maintain required books, with additional penalties per inspection if non-compliance continues.
  • Failure to Install EFD (Electronic Fiscal Device): Businesses required to use EFDs face penalties of BDT 50,000 per device not installed, plus potential sealing of business premises.

sections.h2: VAT Audit Process: What to Expect
sections.content:
The NBR VAT department conducts periodic audits of registered businesses to verify the accuracy of VAT returns and the completeness of records. Understanding the audit process helps you prepare adequately and respond appropriately.

Types of VAT Audits:

  • Routine Audit: Periodic examination of VAT records — typically targeting businesses in high-revenue sectors or those flagged by risk analysis software
  • Intelligence-Based Audit: Triggered by specific information about tax evasion — informant complaints, EFD data anomalies, or cross-matching with customs and income tax data
  • Refund Verification Audit: Conducted before approving large VAT refund claims (especially for exporters)

The Audit Process:

  1. The VAT Commissionerate issues an audit notice specifying the period to be audited and the documents required (typically 15-30 days advance notice)
  2. You must produce all VAT books, invoices, purchase records, bank statements, and contract documents for the specified period
  3. The VAT auditor examines the records and may interview your accounts staff
  4. The auditor issues a Draft Audit Report identifying discrepancies or proposed additional tax demands
  5. You have the right to respond to the draft report and provide explanations within the specified period (usually 15-30 days)
  6. The VAT Commissionerate issues a Final Audit Report and a Demand Notice if tax is found due

Preparation for VAT Audit: Maintain meticulous records, reconcile your VAT returns with your accounting books monthly, and retain all original tax invoices for 5 years. If you are aware of discrepancies before an audit notice arrives, consider a voluntary disclosure to the VAT Commissionerate — this typically results in lower penalties than being caught in an audit.

sections.h2: How to Respond to a VAT Demand Notice
sections.content:
If the NBR issues a VAT demand notice (also called a Tax Recovery Notice), you must take immediate action within the specified timeframe — typically 30 days from the date of notice. Ignoring a VAT demand notice escalates to coercive recovery measures including bank account freezing and asset attachment.

Steps to Take Upon Receiving a VAT Demand Notice:

  1. Read and Understand the Demand: Identify the period, the amount demanded, and the grounds for the demand (late filing, under-declaration, disallowed input credit, etc.).
  2. Consult a VAT Advocate Immediately: This is not the time for DIY — a VAT lawyer or tax consultant can assess whether the demand is legally valid and the best response strategy.
  3. Gather Evidence: Collect all invoices, returns, and records relating to the disputed period. Identify any calculation errors in the NBR's demand.
  4. File a Reply (Objection): Submit a written reply to the VAT Commissionerate explaining why the demand is incorrect and attaching supporting documents. A well-reasoned reply with strong documentary evidence can result in full or partial withdrawal of the demand.
  5. Pay What Is Undisputed: If you agree with part of the demand (e.g., a small late filing penalty), pay that amount to demonstrate good faith and prevent coercive recovery.
  6. File an Appeal if the Reply Is Rejected: If the Commissionerate rejects your reply and upholds the demand, you have the right to appeal to the Commissioner (Appeals) within 30 days of the rejection order.

sections.h2: VAT Appellate Tribunal: Challenging NBR Decisions
sections.content:
When internal NBR appeals (to the Commissioner Appeals) fail, taxpayers can challenge VAT decisions before the independent VAT Appellate Tribunal established under Section 118 of the VAT Act 2012. The Tribunal is presided over by a judicial officer (usually a retired District Judge or senior tax official) and has the power to uphold, modify, or reverse VAT demand decisions.

Appeal Process:

  1. File appeal within 90 days of receiving the Commissioner Appeals' rejection order
  2. Deposit either the full demanded amount or a portion (typically 10%) as security before the appeal is admitted (pre-deposit requirement under Section 116)
  3. Submit grounds of appeal in writing, supported by documentary evidence
  4. The Tribunal hears both parties (taxpayer and NBR) and may call for additional evidence
  5. The Tribunal issues a written order — which is binding on both parties

Further Appeal to the High Court: If the Tribunal's decision is adverse, you can appeal further to the High Court Division of the Supreme Court on questions of law (not facts) within 60 days of the Tribunal's order.

Practical Note: VAT litigation can be expensive and time-consuming. Many businesses prefer to negotiate a settlement with the VAT Commissionerate before the formal appeal process escalates. However, when the NBR's demand is legally unjustified — for example, based on an incorrect interpretation of the law or refusal to allow legitimate input tax credits — pursuing the appeal to the Tribunal and beyond is both justified and often successful.

Our corporate law team at the Uttara, Dhaka chamber of Advocate Md. Shah Alam provides comprehensive VAT advisory and litigation services — from initial registration assistance to defending complex VAT audit cases and representing clients before the VAT Appellate Tribunal. Contact us for expert guidance on all VAT compliance and dispute matters.

faqs.question: What is the current VAT turnover threshold for mandatory registration in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: As of 2026, businesses with annual turnover above BDT 3 crore (30 million) must register for full VAT and file monthly Mushak-9.1 returns. Businesses with turnover between BDT 50 lakh and BDT 3 crore can opt for the simplified Turnover Tax scheme. Businesses below BDT 50 lakh are generally exempt from VAT registration, though mandatory registration may still be required in specific sectors.

faqs.question: What is a BIN number and why is it important?
faqs.answer: A BIN (Business Identification Number) is a unique 9-digit number issued by the NBR upon VAT registration. It is the equivalent of a VAT registration number. Your BIN must be displayed at your business premises, printed on all tax invoices (Mushak-6.1 forms), and used in all VAT correspondence with the NBR. Without a BIN, your customers cannot claim input tax credit on purchases from you.

faqs.question: What is the deadline for filing the monthly VAT return (Mushak-9.1) in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: The Mushak-9.1 monthly VAT return must be filed by the 15th day of the month following the return period. For example, the return for June must be filed by 15 July. Filing after this deadline attracts a penalty of BDT 10,000 for the first month, plus BDT 1,000 per day thereafter, plus 2% monthly interest on the outstanding VAT amount.

faqs.question: Can I claim input tax credit on all my business purchases?
faqs.answer: Not on all purchases. Input tax credit is available only for purchases used in taxable supplies (not for exempt supplies or personal use), supported by a valid tax invoice (Mushak-6.1) from a VAT-registered supplier, and claimed within 4 months of the purchase. You cannot claim input credit on purchases from unregistered suppliers or on goods/services used to make VAT-exempt supplies.

faqs.question: What happens if I receive a VAT demand notice from the NBR?
faqs.answer: You have 30 days from the date of the demand notice to file a reply (objection) to the VAT Commissionerate. Your reply should explain why the demand is incorrect and attach supporting documents. If the Commissionerate rejects your reply, you can appeal to the Commissioner (Appeals) within 30 days and then to the VAT Appellate Tribunal. Do not ignore a VAT demand notice — failure to respond leads to coercive recovery including bank account freezing.

faqs.question: Is VAT applicable on exported goods and services from Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Exported goods and qualifying exported services are zero-rated under the VAT Act 2012, meaning 0% VAT is charged to the foreign buyer. However, the exporter continues to pay input VAT on their local purchases (raw materials, services). The exporter is entitled to claim a refund of this input VAT by filing Form Mushak-19 with the VAT Commissionerate. Refund processing currently takes 3-12 months depending on the commissionerate.
